problem_summary: 2025 MCM Problem B (Sustainable Tourism). The task: build a model for sustainable tourism in Juneau, Alaska (an overtourism case — 1.6M cruise passengers/yr, ~$375M revenue, glacier retreat, congestion, falling resident satisfaction); design a plan to spend additional tax/fee revenue and show how it feeds back to sustainability; run sensitivity analysis to find decisive factors; then extend the model to another overtouristed destination and to promoting undervisited destinations. This O-award paper (2509557) frames the answer as a "triangular balance" of environment, economy, and society and solves it with a coupled system-dynamics ODE model plus a tax-allocation optimization.
models_used: System Dynamics (SD) framework with stock-and-flow structure and explicit positive/negative feedback loops over 4 stocks (tourist volume x, environmental degradation E, infrastructure level I, resident satisfaction S) + tax revenue T as an intermediary flow
Coupled system of 4 first-order ODEs (Eqs 1-4) plus an algebraic tax-revenue equation T = tau*x + f_other*x (Eq 5)

Modified logistic growth model for tourist volume: dx/dt = alpha0(1+lambda_I*I)(1+xi_S*S)x - beta0(1+zeta_E*E)x^2(x/K), where infrastructure and satisfaction boost growth and environmental stress amplifies a super-quadratic crowding/carrying-capacity brake

Linear investment-depreciation dynamics for infrastructure (dI/dt = phi_infra*theta_infra*T - delta_I*I) and a source-recovery-investment balance for environment (dE/dt = eta*x(1+gamma*tau) - delta_E*E - phi_env*theta_env*T)
Linear resident-satisfaction balance: dS/dt = mu_S(I + eps*theta_pub*T) - kappa_S*x - nu_S*E (gains from infra/public service vs. congestion and environmental penalties)
L-BFGS-B (Limited-memory BFGS with Box constraints) to optimize tax-allocation weights theta_env/theta_infra/theta_pub (sum=1) for maximum social welfare
Forward Euler explicit time-stepping integrator (Algorithm 1) to simulate the ODE trajectories
Sensitivity analysis via 2-D parameter sweeps rendered as heatmaps over the 5 government-controllable levers (tau, f_other, theta_env, theta_infra, theta_pub)
key_techniques: Reframing an open policy prompt into a closed-loop feedback system: revenue -> protection -> experience-enhancement -> more revenue, making the 'spend-the-money' sub-question a structural part of the model rather than a bolt-on

Separating the 20 parameters into endogenous dynamics vs. 5 explicitly 'government-controllable' decision variables (tau, f_other, three theta allocations) — this turns the model directly into a policy lever panel
Tying tourist growth to a logistic form justified by post-COVID 'revenge tourism' data, giving the carrying-capacity K a real anchor (2023 cruise volume = 1,650,000)
Using tax revenue T as a single intermediary that couples all four sectors, so one fiscal knob propagates a 'domino effect' through environment, infrastructure, and satisfaction
Layered, color-coded equation annotation (under-braces labeling 'Infrastructure boost', 'Crowding effect', 'Natural recovery', etc.) so each term is interpretable to non-mathematical judges
Heatmap-driven sensitivity that reports concrete deltas (e.g., tau 5%->15% with theta_env=0.40 cuts E by 49.4%, raises tourist suppression to 12.7%) instead of abstract sensitivity coefficients

Translating sensitivity findings into an actionable phased policy: short-term control (raise tau to 11-13%), mid-term improvement (lower tau, raise theta_infra to 40-45%, f_other to $600-700), long-term synergy

Generalization test on a contrasting city (Sanya, China) where tax rate is politically fixed, so only allocation weights are tuned — demonstrating the model adapts to differing policy degrees of freedom
Parameter sourcing table mapping each value to an official report or survey (CBJ Tourism Surveys 2021-2023, budget book) to defend calibration credibility
Including the mandated Memo to the Tourist Council and the AI-use report, with the memo restating the same phased recommendation in plain stakeholder language
why_it_won: "It directly and completely answers every sub-question with a single coherent mechanism. The 'triangular balance' SD model is conceptually clean and memorable, and crucially it makes the second sub-question (how additional revenue is spent and fed back) a *structural feature* of the model via the tax-allocation weights and the revenue->investment->attractiveness->revenue loop, rather than an afterthought. The work is decision-oriented: it isolates exactly five government-controllable levers, optimizes them with a named, citable algorithm (L-BFGS-B), and outputs specific, implementable numbers (raise tax to 6.3%, add $50 entry fee, allocate 0.3/0.4/0.3). The sensitivity analysis is concrete and quantitative (heatmaps with percentage effects), and it is converted into a phased short/mid/long-term roadmap — exactly the kind of governance recommendation judges reward. Generalization is genuine, not cosmetic: extending to Sanya with a *fixed* tax rate shows the model flexes to different policy realities, and the undervisited-destination case closes the loop on the third prompt. Strong narrative framing, well-annotated equations, official-data-backed calibration, and a polished stakeholder memo round out an O-level package."
section_highlights: "Summary (lines 5-9) lands the 'triangular balance' thesis and names the methods (SD, logistic growth, L-BFGS-B) up front. Section 2.1 (lines 177-209) motivates the logistic choice empirically from post-COVID revenge-tourism data and uses multi-source charts to establish the satisfaction decline. Section 4 Notations (line 339) is a single comprehensive table grouping every symbol by equation. Section 5 (lines 343-396) presents the five governing equations with under-brace term-by-term annotation — the analytical core. Section 6 (lines 427-524) is the strongest: it splits parameters into endogenous vs. five controllable levers, states the L-BFGS-B optimization result (0.3/0.4/0.3), gives explicit Euler-integration pseudocode (Algorithm 1), and shows four trajectory plots proving tourists fall below K then recover sustainably. Section 7 (lines 526-624) sensitivity heatmaps with hard numbers, culminating in the phased 'short-term control / mid-term improvement / long-term synergy' strategy. Section 8 (lines 626-709) extends to Sanya (tax fixed, only weights tuned) and to undervisited destinations. The Memo (lines 756-782) re-expresses the recommendation for the Tourist Council."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Internal inconsistency on the headline policy number: the body decides to 'temporarily raise the tax rate to 6.3%' yet the optimal L-BFGS-B allocation is stated as 0.3/0.4/0.3 while the final Solution-of-Juneau recommendation also says 0.3/0.4/0.3 — but the algorithm input/justification for choosing 6.3% specifically is never shown (it is described as 'iterative tuning'), so the optimization claim is partly asserted rather than demonstrated
Unit/scale confusion: x0 is listed as 165x10^6 in Table 2 while K=1,650,000 and all narrative uses ~1.65 million — the initial-condition magnitude is off by ~100x and never reconciled

The crowding term beta0(1+zeta_E*E)x^2*(x/K) is cubic in x (x^2 times x/K), which is a non-standard, ad hoc deviation from the classic logistic (1-x/K) brake; it is presented as an 'improved logistic' but the cubic form is not derived or justified physically

Environmental degradation E goes strongly negative in the output plots (E reaches -4.5 to -5.5 in Fig 8 and -10 in Sanya Fig 14), which is physically meaningless for a 'degradation index' and signals the linear recovery/investment terms are unbounded — no floor/normalization on E
Resident satisfaction S also exceeds its nominal [0,1] range (rises to ~2.9 in Fig 8), again unbounded, undermining interpretability of S as a satisfaction fraction
Many parameter values (alpha0=0.3, eta=0.43, mu_S=0.65, etc.) are 'weighted averages' of survey percentages with opaque derivation; calibration is asserted via citations but not reproducibly shown

Assumptions are heavily linear (linear feedback, constant parameters, immediate investment effect, superposable independent policies) — the paper claims nonlinear feedback in its Advantages/Conclusions (e.g., 'diminishing returns on environmental recovery'), but the actual equations contain no such diminishing-returns nonlinearity, an overclaim
K is admitted to be unknowable and simply fixed to the latest period's value; carrying capacity is the model's most important driver yet least defensible input
Forward Euler with the stiff, super-quadratic dynamics risks numerical instability; no step-size/stability check is reported

Some figure data tables look schematic/illustrative (round numbers, inconsistent year ranges between Juneau 2024-2034 and Sanya 2030-2070) rather than true model output, weakening empirical credibility on close inspection
reusable_patterns: The 'controllable-lever split': after building dynamics, explicitly enumerate the small set of decision variables the stakeholder actually controls, then optimize/sweep only those — instantly makes a descriptive model prescriptive
Use a single intermediary flow (here tax revenue T) to couple otherwise separate sectors so one policy knob produces traceable system-wide effects
Justify the choice of growth law from a real data signature (post-COVID rebound => logistic), rather than asserting it — gives the carrying-capacity model an empirical anchor
Under-brace term annotation on every governing equation, labeling each term's real-world meaning, so judges can read the model without re-deriving it
Convert sensitivity heatmaps into a phased short/mid/long-term action plan with threshold rules (e.g., keep theta_env>=35% so E stays below 0.55) — turns analysis into governance
Validate generalizability on a *contrasting* second case where a key lever is frozen (Sanya's fixed tax rate), proving adaptability rather than re-running the same scenario
Map every parameter to a named official data source in a table to preempt calibration challenges
Cite the optimizer formally (Byrd/Lu/Nocedal/Zhu 1995 for L-BFGS-B) and give explicit integration pseudocode, signaling methodological rigor and reproducibility
Restate the quantitative recommendation in a plain-language stakeholder memo, keeping the numbers identical to the technical body
Structure the whole solution around a single memorable metaphor ('triangular balance of environment, economy, society') that the abstract, body, and conclusion all reinforce
